sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Shortness of breath
History of Present Illness
Lawrence is a 62-year-old male with a past medical history of type I diabetes, congestive heart failure, depression, and reflux. He presents with complaints of shortness of breath, noting that he's been swelling up a little bit and feels lethargic. He reports that the shortness of breath is noticeable when walking up a flight of stairs. He's been experiencing this for approximately 10 days. He also notes some swelling in his legs and reports feeling stiff upon waking, making it difficult to adjust to walking when he gets up. He mentions having experienced shortness of breath once approximately a week ago. He's been monitoring his blood sugar with an insulin pump and has noticed no recent spikes. He's been cutting back on red bull and caffeine due to his reflux. He admits to a fondness for chocolate bars, and acknowledges a challenging year and a half with depression, having tried natural solutions like outdoor stretches but finding it difficult to maintain due to the weather. He is open to medication suggestions for his depression. He has not reported any chest pain, fever, chills, or cough. He denies any belly pain.
Findings

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Physical exam reveals a systolic ejection murmur of 3/6 in the left sternal border, crackles bilaterally at the bases of the lungs, and 1+ pitting edema in the lower extremities. Jugular venous distension is not present. Carotid bruits are absent. The patient's oxygen saturation is stable at 96% on room air.
Assessment
The patient's symptoms and physical exam findings are most consistent with an acute heart failure exacerbation, likely secondary to underlying congestive heart failure and exacerbated by dietary indiscretions.
Plan
I am ordering Lasix 40 milligrams daily and will monitor his weight daily. I will also schedule a follow-up hemoglobin A1c test in one month. I will refer him to psychiatry for further evaluation and management of his depression. I will continue him on omeprazole 20 milligrams daily. I recommend close monitoring and frequent follow-up to assess response to treatment and adjust as needed. If his weight does not improve or if he experiences worsening shortness of breath at rest, I want him to call me or go to the emergency room immediately.
